Name: Peer review and communication
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: recognize the importance of peer review; distinguish between peer-reviewed results and popular media reports.
Description: Peer review is a critical quality-control process that detects false claims and establishes scientific consensus by subjecting research to scrutiny by other experts.

Name: Developing hypotheses
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: use scientific theories and explanations to develop testable hypotheses.
Description: A hypothesis is a proposed explanation for an observation that can be tested through further experimentation and data collection.

Name: Planning and controlling experiments
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: plan experiments; identify independent
Description: dependent
column5: and control variables; explain the need for variables manipulation.
column6: Well-designed investigations require a clear procedure where only one variable is changed at a time to ensure the results are valid and fair.

Name: Selecting apparatus and techniques
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: apply knowledge of techniques and instruments to select those appropriate for an experiment.
Description: Choosing the correct equipment and materials is essential for gathering accurate data and successfully exploring scientific phenomena.

Name: Safety and accuracy
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: carry out experiments safely; correctly manipulate apparatus; identify and reduce hazards.
Description: Practical science requires careful attention to health and safety protocols and the precise use of instruments to ensure reliable measurements.

Name: Sampling techniques
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: recognize when to apply sampling techniques to ensure samples are representative.
Description: Sampling allows scientists to draw conclusions about a whole population by examining a smaller
column5: representative group to avoid bias.

Name: Observations and measurements
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: make and record observations using a range of apparatus; read scales correctly.
Description: Accurate data collection depends on the systematic recording of measurements and qualitative observations using appropriate scientific tools.

Name: Evaluating methods
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: evaluate methods and suggest improvements; assess if measurements are sufficient and precise.
Description: Critical evaluation of an experimental design identifies weaknesses and suggests refinements to ensure the findings are robust and valid.

Name: Presenting data
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: present observations using frequency tables
Description: bar charts
column5: and histograms; plot variables on graphs.
column6: Data must be organized and visualized clearly to allow for easier identification of patterns and relationships between different variables.

Name: Translating data
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: translate data between graphical and numeric forms.
Description: Information must be accurately converted between different formats
column5: such as turning a table of results into a representative line or bar graph.

Name: Mathematical and statistical analysis
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: calculate means; use significant figures; change the subject of equations; determine slopes and intercepts.
Description: Scientific analysis involves applying mathematical tools to find averages
column5: calculate rates of change
column6: and manipulate algebraic formulas.

Name: Uncertainty and estimations
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: represent distributions of results; make estimations of uncertainty using the range about the mean.
Description: Every scientific measurement carries some degree of uncertainty
column5: which can be estimated by looking at the spread of data points around the average value.

Name: Interpreting data
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: identify patterns and trends; make inferences and draw conclusions from verbal or numerical data.
Description: Analysis involves looking beyond the raw numbers to determine what the data actually means and whether it supports a specific conclusion.

Name: Relating data to hypotheses
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: present reasoned explanations; comment on the consistency of data with a given hypothesis.
Description: Scientists must determine if their experimental findings support their original predictions or if a different hypothesis provides a better explanation.

Name: Evaluating data quality
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: evaluate accuracy
Description: precision
column5: repeatability
column6: and reproducibility; identify random and systematic errors.
column7: High-quality data is close to the true value (accurate) and consistent (precise)
column8: and it should be achievable by different people using different equipment (reproducible).

Name: Communicating rationale
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: present coherent and logically structured reports using various symbolic and numerical forms.
Description: Scientific findings must be communicated clearly through structured reports that explain the reasoning
column5: methods
column6: and final conclusions of an investigation.

Name: Vocabulary
Learning Outcomes: quantities
Description: and units
column5: Students should be able to: use scientific terminology; apply SI units and prefixes; use powers of ten; use appropriate significant figures.
column6: Scientific communication relies on a standardized system of units
